sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A clinic evaluated patients with panic disorder who completed either cognitive behavioral therapy alone or CBT plus interoceptive exposure. Symptom severity was rated on a 0–40 scale before treatment and after 8 weeks. The CBT-only group changed from 28 to 17, while the CBT-plus-exposure group changed from 29 to 12. Patients in the exposure group practiced safely inducing feared bodily sensations such as dizziness or rapid heartbeat in order to learn that those sensations were not dangerous.
questions.stem: Which group had the lower post-treatment symptom score?

choices.B: Both groups had identical post-treatment scores

explanations.C: Correct. The exposure group ended at 12, lower than the CBT-only group at 17. Since lower scores indicate fewer symptoms, it had the better post-treatment outcome.
explanations.A: The passage does not support cbt only; it points instead to cbt plus interoceptive exposure.
explanations.B: The passage does not support both groups had identical post-treatment scores; it points instead to cbt plus interoceptive exposure.
explanations.D: The passage does not support neither group improved; it points instead to cbt plus interoceptive exposure.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Panic symptom severity before and after CBT alone or CBT plus interoceptive exposure.

questions.stem: Why is interoceptive exposure useful in panic disorder?

choices.A: It helps patients learn that feared bodily sensations are uncomfortable but not dangerous
choices.B: It prevents all bodily arousal forever
choices.C: It teaches avoidance of every elevated heartbeat

explanations.A: Correct. Panic disorder often involves catastrophic interpretation of bodily sensations. Safely practicing those sensations can reduce fear of them and weaken the panic cycle.
explanations.B: The passage does not support it prevents all bodily arousal forever; it points instead to it helps patients learn that feared bodily sensations are uncomfortable but not dangerous.
explanations.C: The passage does not support it teaches avoidance of every elevated heartbeat; it points instead to it helps patients learn that feared bodily sensations are uncomfortable but not dangerous.
explanations.D: The passage does not support it works by increasing hallucinations; it points instead to it helps patients learn that feared bodily sensations are uncomfortable but not dangerous.

questions.stem: Why should causal language be used cautiously for this passage?

choices.D: The design leaves room for alternative explanations or selection effects
choices.A: The passage contains no measurable variables
choices.B: All participant reports are automatically false
choices.C: The topic is unrelated to P/S content

explanations.D: Correct. If patients chose their treatment, motivation or willingness to practice exposure could differ between groups and partly explain the larger improvement.
explanations.A: The passage includes concrete measures or observations, so this is incorrect.
explanations.B: Self-report can have limitations, but it is not automatically false.
explanations.C: The topic fits psychological, social, or behavioral science content.

questions.stem: Which application best follows from the passage?

choices.B: Practice feared sensations in a controlled setting while learning new interpretations
choices.A: Ignore the social or behavioral context described
choices.C: Use a one-size-fits-all intervention even when the passage shows different barriers
choices.D: Assume the measured pattern cannot be changed

explanations.B: Correct. This matches interoceptive exposure: evoking sensations safely and pairing them with noncatastrophic interpretations.
explanations.A: Ignoring the described context would miss the mechanism emphasized in the passage.
explanations.C: A uniform intervention would not address the specific pattern described.
explanations.D: The passage supports practical response rather than fatalism.

choices.C: CBT plus exposure was associated with greater improvement, but assignment details matter for causal strength
choices.A: The finding proves a universal law for every person
choices.B: The main construct is irrelevant because one limitation exists
choices.D: The passage supports only biological explanations

explanations.C: Correct. The symptom change supports the treatment pattern while preserving caution about whether group selection influenced results.
explanations.A: The passage supports a bounded interpretation, not a universal absolute.
explanations.B: A limitation weakens overclaiming but does not make the construct irrelevant.
explanations.D: P/S passages often involve social and psychological mechanisms beyond biology alone.

questions.passage: A campus mental-health survey asked students whether they would seek counseling if they experienced persistent low mood, sleep disturbance, and loss of interest. Students who endorsed higher perceived public stigma were less likely to say they would seek help. Several students said they worried that friends would think they were weak. Others said that hearing peers openly discuss counseling made treatment seem more normal. The researchers concluded that stigma can reduce help-seeking by shaping perceived social costs.
questions.stem: What relationship does the survey describe?

choices.D: Higher perceived public stigma was associated with lower willingness to seek counseling
choices.A: Higher stigma increased willingness to seek counseling
choices.C: Every student said they would seek counseling immediately

explanations.D: Correct. The passage states that students with higher perceived public stigma were less willing to seek help. That is the central association.
explanations.A: The passage does not support higher stigma increased willingness to seek counseling; it points instead to higher perceived public stigma was associated with lower willingness to seek counseling.
explanations.B: The passage does not support stigma was unrelated to help-seeking; it points instead to higher perceived public stigma was associated with lower willingness to seek counseling.
explanations.C: The passage does not support every student said they would seek counseling immediately; it points instead to higher perceived public stigma was associated with lower willingness to seek counseling.

questions.stem: Worrying that friends would think one is weak is best described as what?

explanations.A: Correct. The student expects negative judgment if symptoms or counseling are disclosed, which is anticipated stigma.
explanations.B: The passage does not support crystallized intelligence; it points instead to anticipated stigma.
explanations.C: The passage does not support habituation; it points instead to anticipated stigma.
explanations.D: The passage does not support the vestibular sense; it points instead to anticipated stigma.

questions.stem: Why should causal language be used cautiously for this passage?

choices.B: The design leaves room for alternative explanations or selection effects
choices.A: The passage contains no measurable variables
choices.C: All participant reports are automatically false
choices.D: The topic is unrelated to P/S content

explanations.B: Correct. The survey measured stigma and intentions at the same time without manipulating stigma, so it supports association rather than definitive causation.
explanations.A: The passage includes concrete measures or observations, so this is incorrect.
explanations.C: Self-report can have limitations, but it is not automatically false.
explanations.D: The topic fits psychological, social, or behavioral science content.

questions.stem: Which application best follows from the passage?

choices.C: Normalize counseling and address fears about social judgment
choices.A: Ignore the social or behavioral context described
choices.B: Use a one-size-fits-all intervention even when the passage shows different barriers
choices.D: Assume the measured pattern cannot be changed

explanations.C: Correct. The passage indicates that social costs reduce help-seeking, so normalizing treatment directly targets that barrier.
explanations.A: Ignoring the described context would miss the mechanism emphasized in the passage.
explanations.B: A uniform intervention would not address the specific pattern described.
explanations.D: The passage supports practical response rather than fatalism.

choices.A: Stigma can reduce willingness to seek help by changing perceived social consequences
choices.B: The finding proves a universal law for every person
choices.C: The main construct is irrelevant because one limitation exists
choices.D: The passage supports only biological explanations

explanations.A: Correct. This conclusion is directly supported by the student comments and the stigma-help-seeking association.
explanations.B: The passage supports a bounded interpretation, not a universal absolute.
explanations.C: A limitation weakens overclaiming but does not make the construct irrelevant.
explanations.D: P/S passages often involve social and psychological mechanisms beyond biology alone.
